first_paragraph: [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") has been a meeting area of the [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"), [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages"), [Romance](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), and [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") linguistic and cultural regions,[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6) which makes it one of the most complex meeting point of languages in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[[6]](#cite_note-7) The [official](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") and [national](/wiki/National_language "National language") language of Slovenia is [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), which is spoken by a large majority of the population. It is also known, in English, as Slovenian. Two minority languages, namely [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), are recognised as co-official languages and accordingly protected in their residential municipalities.[[7]](#cite_note-Constitution-8) Other significant languages are [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language") and its variants and [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language"), spoken by most immigrants from [other countries of former Yugoslavia](/wiki/Former_Yugoslavia "Former Yugoslavia") and their descendants. Slovenia is ranked among the top European countries regarding the knowledge of foreign languages. The most often taught foreign languages are [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), followed by Italian, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language").[[8]](#cite_note-MLD_2010-9)

[Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") has been a meeting area of the [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"), [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages"), [Romance](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), and [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") linguistic and cultural regions,[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6) which makes it one of the most complex meeting point of languages in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[[6]](#cite_note-7) The [official](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") and [national](/wiki/National_language "National language") language of Slovenia is [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), which is spoken by a large majority of the population. It is also known, in English, as Slovenian. Two minority languages, namely [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), are recognised as co-official languages and accordingly protected in their residential municipalities.[[7]](#cite_note-Constitution-8) Other significant languages are [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language") and its variants and [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language"), spoken by most immigrants from [other countries of former Yugoslavia](/wiki/Former_Yugoslavia "Former Yugoslavia") and their descendants. Slovenia is ranked among the top European countries regarding the knowledge of foreign languages. The most often taught foreign languages are [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), followed by Italian, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language").[[8]](#cite_note-MLD_2010-9)

The population of Slovenia has become more diverse in regard to its language through recent decades but is still relatively homogenous — [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language") was in 2002 the first language of 87.8% of the inhabitants.[[8]](#cite_note-MLD_2010-9)[[9]](#cite_note-Strubelj-10) It was followed by [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language") (2.8%), [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language") (1.6%) and [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian") (1.6%). The Italian and Hungarian languages are protected by the Constitution of Slovenia, but have lower numbers of native speakers.[[9]](#cite_note-Strubelj-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

## Slovene

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Slovene")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/81/Slovenian_language_map.png)](/wiki/File:Slovenian_language_map.png)

Areas where Slovene is spoken

In its Article 11, the [Constitution of Slovenia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Slovenia "Constitution of Slovenia") stipulates that Slovene is to be the sole official and national language throughout the country. The Public Use of the Slovene Language Act of 2004[[11]](#cite_note-12) further defines the legal status of Slovene, by mandating that national and local authorities are compelled to use it in communication and legislation. As a national language, it is used on the obverse side of [Slovenian euro coins](/wiki/Slovenian_euro_coins "Slovenian euro coins"), in the [Slovenian national anthem](/wiki/Zdravljica "Zdravljica"), by [The Slovenian President](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_Slovenia "List of Presidents of Slovenia"), and uniquely represents [Slovene culture](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") on the international stage.

Television and radio broadcasts, newspapers, commercials, user manuals, and other printed or broadcast material must be in Slovene. Usage of material in another language is permitted, if it is accordingly subtitled, dubbed or translated. Publishing or broadcasting untranslated material, as well as selling goods without instructions and declaration in Slovene, is punishable and banned by law. Also, names of corporations and trademarks registered in Slovenia must be in Slovene; however, they may be used along with the translated name in another language if its aimed at foreign markets.

Slovene is the language of instruction at all levels of schooling, from primary to tertiary education. There is an international high school in [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana") with English as the language of instruction, but it admits only students from foreign diplomats and Slovenes who had been schooled abroad for several years. University programs are offered in Slovene but a subset are also offered in foreign languages such as English.

The Centre for Slovene as a Second/Foreign Language[[12]](#cite_note-13) encourages the learning of Slovene as foreign language, offers different courses in Slovene, and grants certificates of language proficiency. One may sit for the Slovene Language Exam at three levels: Basic, Intermediate and Advanced. After having passed the exam, the applicant receives the certificate of knowledge of the Slovene language, issued by the Faculty of Arts of [University of Ljubljana](/wiki/University_of_Ljubljana "University of Ljubljana"), which is valid throughout the European Union, and makes the holder eligible to apply to any school or university in Slovenia.

With the accession of Slovenia to the European Union on May 1, 2004, Slovene became an official language of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), requiring that all Acts and Directives be translated into Slovene. Additionally, Slovenian citizens may write to any EU institution in Slovene and expect a response in the same language.

### Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Dialects")]

Main article: [Slovene dialects](/wiki/Slovene_dialects "Slovene dialects")

Slovene is divided into seven regional dialectal groups, further subdivided into local dialects. Mutual comprehension between certain dialects is limited.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

#### Prekmurje Slovene

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prekmurje Slovene")]

Main article: [Prekmurje Slovene](/wiki/Prekmurje_Slovene "Prekmurje Slovene")

This is the only Slovene dialect that has ever been attempted to be declared an official language in the Prekmurje region.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[14]](#cite_note-15) It has a limited standardized written form,[[15]](#cite_note-16) has been used in the liturgy,[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) and has been used in modern literature, music, television and film.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Ethnic minority languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ethnic minority languages")]

### Italian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Italian")]

Main article: [Italian language in Slovenia](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Slovenia "Italian language in Slovenia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Piran_-_bilingual_signs%21_%281621587289%29.jpg/250px-Piran_-_bilingual_signs%21_%281621587289%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piran_-_bilingual_signs!_(1621587289).jpg)

Bilingual Slovene-Italian sign in [Piran](/wiki/Piran "Piran").

The [Italian language is an officially recognized minority language](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Slovenia "Italian language in Slovenia") in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"). Around 3,700 Slovenian citizens speak Italian as their [mother tongue](/wiki/Mother_tongue "Mother tongue"), mostly [Istrian Italians](/wiki/Istrian_Italians "Istrian Italians"). Their numbers drastically decreased following the [Istrian–Dalmatian exodus](/wiki/Istrian%E2%80%93Dalmatian_exodus "Istrian–Dalmatian exodus") (1943–1960).

Italian is officially recognised as the mother tongue of the protected Italian minority and co-official language in [Slovenian Istria](/wiki/Slovenian_Istria "Slovenian Istria") near the Slovenian-Italian border and at the Slovenian coastline. Public usage of Italian is permitted and protected by minority protection laws. Members of the Italian minority are entitled to primary and secondary education in their native language, as well as to radio and television programmes in Italian, and to communicating in Italian with the authorities.

Italian is co-official with Slovene in 25 settlements in 4 municipalities (all of them officially bilingual):

### Hungarian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hungarian")]

Hungarian is officially recognised as the mother tongue of protected Hungarian minority in [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje") region near the Slovenian-Hungarian border. Public usage of Hungarian is permitted and protected by minority protection laws. Members of Hungarian minority are entitled to primary and secondary education in their native language, as well to radio and occasional television broadcast in Hungarian, and to communicating in Hungarian with the authorities.

Hungarian is co-official with Slovene in 30 settlements in 5 municipalities (whereof 3 are officially bilingual):

* **Municipality of Dobrovnik** (Občina Dobrovnik, *hung.* Dobronak Község)
  + [Dobrovnik](/wiki/Dobrovnik "Dobrovnik"), *hung.* Dobronak

### South Slavic languages and Albanian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: South Slavic languages and Albanian")]

A significant number of Slovenian population speak a variant of [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language") and [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language") as their native language. These are mostly immigrants who moved to Slovenia from other [former Yugoslav](/wiki/Former_Yugoslav "Former Yugoslav") republics from the 1960s to the late 1980s, and their descendants. 0.4% of the Slovenian population declared themselves as native speakers of [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language") and 0.2% as native speakers of [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonian_language "Macedonian language") in 2002.[[20]](#cite_note-stat.si-21)

There is a small Croatian speaking community in [White Carniola](/wiki/White_Carniola "White Carniola"), whose existence predates Yugoslavia. In four villages ([Miliči](/wiki/Mili%C4%8Di "Miliči"), [Bojanci](/wiki/Bojanci "Bojanci"), [Marindol](/wiki/Marindol "Marindol") and [Paunoviči](/wiki/Paunovi%C4%8Di "Paunoviči")), people speak the [Eastern Herzegovinian dialect](/wiki/Eastern_Herzegovinian_dialect "Eastern Herzegovinian dialect"), with a strong influence of Slovene. People living there are mainly [Serbian Orthodox](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox "Serbian Orthodox") and descendants of Serbian [Uskoks](/wiki/Uskoks "Uskoks"), guerilla warriors against Ottoman invasions.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

In its sixth evaluation report on Slovenia's implementation of the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages"), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") urged the country once again to recognise Serbian and Croatian as traditional minority languages.[[22]](#cite_note-BI-Serbian/Croatian/German-23) The 2025 call followed earlier recommendations made in 2014, 2019 and 2021.[[22]](#cite_note-BI-Serbian/Croatian/German-23)

## Romani

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Romani")]

[Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language"),[[23]](#cite_note-24) spoken in 2002 as the native language by 0.2% of people, is a legally protected language in Slovenia. These people mainly belong to the geographically dispersed and marginalized Roma community.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

## German

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: German")]

[German](/wiki/German_language "German language") and [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") dialects have been autochthonous since present-day Slovenia came under the rule of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") in the 8th century. Whereas many immigrants from German-speaking areas adopted Slovene over the centuries, others retained their language. Until the 20th century, the most numerous German-speaking communities were found in the urban centers of [Lower Styria](/wiki/Lower_Styria "Lower Styria"), in the [Gottschee County](/wiki/Gottschee "Gottschee") in southern Slovenia and in the villages around [Apače](/wiki/Apa%C4%8De "Apače") ([Apaško polje](https://sl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apa%C5%A1ko_polje "sl:Apaško polje")) along the [Mura](/wiki/Mur_(river) "Mur (river)") river.

According to the last Austrian census of 1910, around 9% of the population of present-day Slovenia spoke German as their native tongue. Towns with a German-speaking majority included [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor"), [Celje](/wiki/Celje "Celje"), [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj"), [Kočevje](/wiki/Ko%C4%8Devje "Kočevje"), [Slovenj Gradec](/wiki/Slovenj_Gradec "Slovenj Gradec"), [Slovenska Bistrica](/wiki/Slovenska_Bistrica "Slovenska Bistrica"), [Ormož](/wiki/Ormo%C5%BE "Ormož"), [Dravograd](/wiki/Dravograd "Dravograd") and some other smaller towns.

After World War I, the number of German-speakers dropped significantly: most of the towns were slovenized, and German remained the majority language only in the Gottschee County and around Apače. According to the last pre-WWII Yugoslav census, German speakers amounted to 2.5% of the overall population. Former German or bilingual speakers had switched to Slovene, the official language.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), ethnic Germans were resettled from areas occupied by Italy ([Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana"), [Gottschee](/wiki/Gottschee "Gottschee")) into the German-occupied zone. On the end and after the war, the great majority of the remaining Germans were expropriated (AVNOJ-Decree), expulsed or murdered by Yugoslav partisans. In the census of 2002, just 1,628 persons (0.1% of the population) declared German as their mother tongue. Almost everyone today born in Slovenia knows Slovene because people learn obligatory Slovene in school, but many at home speak other languages as well. The number of people fluent in German is unknown. German-speaking women around Maribor who are citizens of Slovenia have organized in the association *Kulturno društvo nemško govorečih žena »Mostovi« Maribor* ("Bridges").[[25]](#cite_note-26)

### Gottscheerish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Gottscheerish")]

[Gottscheerish](/wiki/Gottscheerish "Gottscheerish") or Granish is a [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") dialect of the [German language](/wiki/German_language "German language") and has been spoken in the [Gottschee County](/wiki/Gottschee "Gottschee") around [Kočevje](/wiki/Ko%C4%8Devje "Kočevje") (Gottschee) since 1330. For over 600 years, it was the predominant oral language of the [Gottscheers](/wiki/Gottscheers "Gottscheers") in that area, whereas [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") was their written language. Most Gottschee Germans were resettled by the German occupation forces in 1941 during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Only a few hundred Gottscheers remained, most of them supporting the partisan movement. After the war, Gottscheerish was forbidden. Today there are only a few speakers left, most of them in Moschnitze valley (Črmošnjiško-Poljanska dolina) between [Kočevske Poljane](/wiki/Ko%C4%8Devske_Poljane "Kočevske Poljane") and [Črmošnjice](/wiki/%C4%8Crmo%C5%A1njice,_Semi%C4%8D "Črmošnjice, Semič"), others today mainly know standard German.[[26]](#cite_note-pmk20-27)[[27]](#cite_note-unesco-gottscheer-28)[[28]](#cite_note-norwaysi-29)

### Recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Recognition")]

Gottscheerish and German, though autochthonous to Slovenia, have no official status and are not protected by law.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

## Czech and Slovak

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Czech and Slovak")]

[Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language") and [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language"), which used to be the fourth largest minority language in Slovenia prior to World War II (after Italian, German, Hungarian, Croatian and Serbian), is now the native languages of a few hundred Slovenian residents.[[20]](#cite_note-stat.si-21)

## Foreign languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Foreign languages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Foreign_languages_Slovenia.jpg/500px-Foreign_languages_Slovenia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Foreign_languages_Slovenia.jpg)

The most commonly known foreign languages in Slovenia in 2005. According to [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat").[[30]](#cite_note-Eurostat-31)

Historically, German was the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* of [Central European](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") space and was perceived as the language of commerce, science and literature in Slovenia. Consequently, German used to be the first foreign language taught in schools. With the formation of Yugoslavia, [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian") became the language of federal authorities and the first foreign language taught in school. Slovenes pick up Serbo-Croatian more easily than other foreign languages due to its relative linguistic similarity to Slovene, as both form the [South Slavic dialect continuum](/wiki/South_Slavic_dialect_continuum "South Slavic dialect continuum").

Nowadays, English has superseded it and is taught as the first foreign language throughout the country from pre-school onwards. German has, however, retained its strong position as an important language and is the most common second foreign language in high schools. According to Eurostat, Slovenia has the highest competence in German of any non-Germanic country, with only Luxembourg, the Netherlands, and Denmark being higher.[[30]](#cite_note-Eurostat-31) Other second foreign languages are Italian, [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and Hungarian. Among the five subjects in the Slovenian finishing exam ([Matura](/wiki/Matura "Matura")), one foreign language—most commonly English—is compulsory.

As a consequence of different foreign languages having been taught at different times, there is no prevailing foreign language knowledge in Slovenia. Younger generations speak English and Serbo-Croatian well enough to communicate, whereas elder generations generally speak Serbo-Croatian and German better. There are also regional differences, especially among the knowledge of a second foreign language, with German being more frequently taught and used in [Styria](/wiki/Lower_Styria "Lower Styria") region, whereas residents of the [Littoral](/wiki/Slovenian_Littoral "Slovenian Littoral") region have better familiarity with Italian. In Prekmurje region, [Prekmurje dialect](/wiki/Prekmurje_Slovene "Prekmurje Slovene") sees a degree of mixing with [Kajkavian Croatian](/wiki/Kajkavian "Kajkavian"), while in [Gorizia](/wiki/Gorizia_Statistical_Region "Gorizia Statistical Region") on the border with Italy, [Littoral Slovene dialects](/wiki/Littoral_dialect_group "Littoral dialect group") see Italian, Venetian and Friulian influence.
